title: Deathnote
author: Anonymous
variants:
Elias Renn.


A man three carriages back swore this works, so I'm writing the name above before I lose my nerve.

You take a book and quill. On the first page, the name. Only the name — nothing else, he said, or it won't take. Then you sign it. That's the part that matters. The signing.

After that you just have to die. Somewhere ahead of them, out on the tracks, your echo climbs up and goes looking for the name you wrote.

I've written it. My hand won't sign yet. What if nothing happens? What if something does?

Marlow Pike

I don't believe in it. I want that on the record.

But I watched a woman sign one of these, and I watched her walk off the roof two carriages later, and three days after that a thing wearing her face came marching down the line asking for a man named Corvin.

Corvin is not on this train anymore. I don't know if he got off or if he was got.

So now I'm sitting here with my own book and quill and the name up top that I'd rather not say aloud. I'm telling myself I'm only testing it. I'm telling myself a lot of things.

Sabine Thorne.


For whoever finds this:

It's a curse you carry, not one you cast. You write the name — hers is on the page before this — you sign the book, and then it just waits inside you. Costs you nothing until the moment you fall. Then it spends everything at once.

They say the echo knows exactly where you died and starts from there, hunting forward until it reaches the one you named. I don't know how it crosses between us. I don't know if the name even has to belong to someone real.

I signed mine an hour ago. I feel completely ordinary. That's the part I can't stop thinking about.

Whit Calloway

Faulthurst told me not to. He said the train keeps its accounts and a Death Note is a debt you sign in your own blood.

I asked him if it actually functions. He looked at me for a long time and said, 'That is the wrong question. The right question is whether you can live with it working.'

I have written the name at the top. I have not signed it. The quill is right here.

If you are reading this in a chest, it means I put the book down. Take that as advice or take it as cowardice. I no longer know which it is.

Dorne Vasco.


Test log, attempt one.

Hypothesis: writing the passenger's name on the first page of a book — done, see the previous page — and signing it binds a curse to me. On my next death aboard the train, an echo of me spawns ahead and pursues the named passenger.

Method: I wrote the name. I signed it. The book went black and burned in my hands, which was NOT in the instructions and which I did not enjoy.

Result: pending. Obviously. I have to die to collect the data, and I find I am suddenly very attached to not doing that.

Conclusion: unknown. If you are reading this, the experiment is still running. Be careful whose name you'd sign. Be more careful about mine.
